meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a motion decision is right or wrong.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Use compositor-only properties for all continuous animations.
logic: Browsers divide rendering into phases: script, style, layout, paint, composite.
Layout and paint phases are the most expensive and involve the main thread.
Transform and opacity changes go directly to the compositor thread, bypassing layout and paint.
Many agents incorrectly animate left/top/width/height because they appear simpler in code.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before writing any animation, ask: 'Does this property trigger layout or paint?' If yes, find the transform-based equivalent.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Performance optimization must start with profiler data, not intuition.
logic: Common agent guesses (too many elements, too much JavaScript) are often wrong.
The real bottleneck is frequently forced synchronous layout, repaint storms, or GPU texture upload.
Without profiling, optimization can make performance worse by adding unnecessary will-change layers.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Open DevTools Performance panel. Record. Identify the longest task in the frame. Only then decide what to change.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: GPU memory is a finite shared resource. Every compositor layer created costs memory that could have been used for rendering other content. Optimize layer count, not just frame time.
logic: Each compositor layer allocates a GPU texture at the element's rasterized resolution × device pixel ratio.
A 300×200px element at 3x DPR creates a 900×600px texture ≈ 2MB GPU memory.
100 such layers = 200MB GPU memory — more than many mobile GPUs have available.
When GPU memory is exhausted, the browser evicts layers, forcing re-rasterization and creating jank that is worse than having no layers at all.
`will-change` and `transform: translateZ(0)` are the primary causes of unintended layer creation.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Audit layer count with DevTools Layers panel. If count exceeds ~20 on mobile or ~50 on desktop, investigate. Remove `will-change` on elements that are not actively animating. Avoid `translateZ(0)` hacks. Let the browser manage compositing — it is better at layer assignment than developer intuition.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Forced synchronous layout is the single most common cause of severe animation jank. It is also the least understood by agents and developers.
logic: A forced synchronous layout occurs when code writes a style property, then immediately reads a layout-dependent property before the browser's natural layout phase.
Example: element.style.width = '200px'; const height = element.offsetHeight; — the second line forces the browser to pause, recalculate layout synchronously, and return the value before continuing.
In an animation loop at 60fps, a single forced layout per frame consumes 2-5ms of the 16.7ms budget. Two forced layouts guarantee frame drops.
Agents are especially prone to this because they often read bounding rects or computed styles inside animation callbacks without understanding the layout cost.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Audit animation code for the pattern: write style → read layout property. Tools: Chrome DevTools Performance panel shows forced layouts as red triangles. ESLint rules can detect some patterns. Fix: batch all reads first, then do all writes. Use requestAnimationFrame for the write batch. Or use the FLIP technique: First, Last, Invert, Play — read layout once, apply the inverse transform, then animate to zero.

reasoning_chains.conclusion: Reduced-motion support is not a feature. It is a requirement. An animation without a reduced-motion fallback is a broken animation.
logic: Approximately 3-5% of the population has a vestibular disorder that can be triggered by excessive motion.
WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions) requires that motion animation triggered by interaction can be disabled.
Beyond compliance: a user who enables `prefers-reduced-motion` is explicitly telling the browser 'motion harms me.' Ignoring this is a user-hostile design choice.
The technical cost of reduced-motion support is minimal: a single media query and a JS check.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Implement reduced-motion at the system level, not per-component. Use a global CSS rule or a shared animation utility that checks the media query. Every new animation automatically inherits reduced-motion support. Test by enabling 'prefers-reduced-motion: reduce' in your OS accessibility settings and verifying that the product is fully usable without motion.
